item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want that rubber frog that misquotes Samuel Beckett in the voice of Vincent Price. I MUST HAVE THAT FROG.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: HA. Nice Homestuck reference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Surprised no one got the Ace of Bass reference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While this wasn't particularly my cup of tea overall, I would be remiss if I did not advise you that you that you have a golden opportunity to add a copy of 173 to the log with a repeated warning "I warned you about those stares dog!" for the rarely seen or attempted SBAHJ refrancex2combo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. You had an interesting concept here, what with an item created for the sole purpose of wasting the foundations time, but the fourth wall breaking completely undermines everything else about the article and comes absolutely out of fucking nowhere. I don't usually say stuff like this, but if you take out the fourth wall break I'll upvote. For now however, it's a definite -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh god the jokes become canon and vice versa. And I like it.
It begins… (Im predicting taumiel ducks)

Honestly: funny and serious. Good. Especially like the log.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is a clarity issue here that I think got muddled over with all of the new-CI-shennanigans.

That being: Does the Foundation keep discovering new instances of 2215 when they mention CI-affiliated items (e.g., they say CI-314, and they point to an undiscovered 2215 instance that happens to be the closest to CI-314)? Or did they just do testing in which they spaced the 2215 instances around the world? Does the closest 2215 instance to the CI object, the one with the text, point at anything?

I'm all for shenanigans, but I still need to understand what is happening with the object. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The intent was to show my opinion on the silliness of such concerns- the whole "this is too silly to be a mainlist" nonsense. This is my interpretation of being "out and proud" about the thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a strange means of doing it, then. My vote would remain the same in any case, but I'd prefer the thing to be out and proud about what it's doing rather than giving the reader this sort of self-conscious worrying about how tone is going to be perceived. Seeing as how the intent was to call further attention to how silly this is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not meant to negate anything. I actually like my SCPs silly, hard as that may be to believe. This is meant to highlight that fact, not obfuscate it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, this probably was going to earn a downvote in any event for being far too silly for me to be able to stomach on a personal level. I've never thought, though, that a work being self-aware that it's doing a thing ever really negates that thing. I found that part of it to be actively obnoxious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno, the interview was a little too on-the-nose for my tastes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: best scp

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I'm with Scantron. I don't like the idea of magical "find the other guys' stuff" signs in the first place, and then the interview was, for whatever, meta-commentary on article posting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't feel the need to take tales seriously if the tone and content don't warrant it, but I do need to be able to take mainlist SCPs seriously. This version of the CI was fun to read in that tale, but it just doesn't fit in with the site's more serious content by virtue of its fourth wall-breaking shit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems like an odd distinction, but I suppose it's yours to make.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this version of the CI in a tale, but I can't take them seriously in a mainlist SCP article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As I mentioned, there is more of this sort of CI stuff in the tale I mentioned above. If interest arises in this interpretation of the GoI, I might write more of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am unsure about this, probably because I don't know the CI (or this canon) very much at all. I was okay with the concept, then minded to downvote for the 087 reference (because the idea that one of my favourite skips can just be duplicated makes it so mundane that my headcanon forcibly rejected the thought). And then read the interview, and felt like I was being told off for feeling that way!

No vote for now. I suspect that once I understand it better, I will upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only interesting part for me is the interview, but that really makes it some how. Especially 'We are being read' and the tone references. It feels like a sapient story, not a group of people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The CI items are an interesting add-on, but the real kicker is in the interview. It feels so fourth-wall-breaking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Digging this new direction the CI is taking. +1'd and hope to see more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some of them are, others are likely just stuff I missed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure the tone is quite right… :)

There are a few grammatical errors in it, is this on purpose?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some more neo-CI stuff, much like they appeared in the tale Chasing Interest. Hope you enjoy it.
